source.summary: Cerulean is validating Redwood's data handling controls: no-training guarantees, prompt retention and deletion, customer-managed keys (BYOK/HSM), audit log retention and tamper-evidence, SSO/RBAC mapping, and data residency options. Agreed to exchange SOC2, KMS integration notes, and run a short pilot with synthetic PHI to validate operational behavior.
topics: No-training on customer data / model training guarantees
Prompt & response retention windows, redact/ephemeral modes
Audit logs: retention, tamper-evidence, auditor access
SSO/SAML, RBAC roles and least privilege
source.transcript:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-04-18 15:00 PDT
Duration: 58 minutes (approx)
Attendees: Maya (Redwood AE), Jamal (Redwood SE), Priya (Redwood Sec), Lisa (CISO - Cerulean), Daniel (Head of MLOps), Anika (Privacy Counsel)

Auto-generated summary (may be imperfect): Cerulean asked for assurance that Redwood will not use customer prompts or outputs for model training, details on how long prompts are retained and how to enforce deletion, how BYOK works with KMS/HSM, audit log integrity and retention, RBAC mapping to their IdP, and options for data residency. Redwood explained hosted vs dedicated differences and offered pilot with synthetic PHI. Next steps captured.

Transcript body:
[00:00] Maya: hey everyone thanks for carving out the time we really appreciate it. quick roll call then i can hand off to jamal and priya for the deeper bits.
[00:12] Lisa: Lisa Park here, CISO at Cerulean, hi.
[00:15] Daniel: Daniel Reeves, MLOps.
[00:18] Anika: Anika, privacy counsel.
[00:20] Maya: perfect, jamal - wanna kick off on retention and training policy?
[00:25] Jamal: yeah sure. so high level — we have a formal no-train commitment by default for customer data on hosted tiers. that means prompts and outputs are not incorporated into model training corpora. we do have internal telemetry for perf tuning but that is pseudonymized and never used to push back into training sets without explicit opt-in.
[00:40] Daniel: when you say "no-train by default" is that contractual? we need it written down, and also what about subcontractors or partners — like if you use a vendor for embeddings or caching?
[00:52] Priya: good question. we cover that in the data processing addendum — the no-train statement is in the DPA for enterprise customers and for Dedicated/Private deployments we can provide a written no-training attest. subcontractors are enumerated in the contract — if anything changes we notify customers and there are contractual controls.
[01:10] Anika: okay but how do you technically prevent training? do you scrub or delete logs? is there a window?
[01:17] Jamal: we have configurable retention policies. default hosted retention is 30 days for prompts and responses, then we purge. for enterprise customers you can set shorter windows or ephemeral mode which deletes after 24 hours. for Dedicated you get full control and we can enable ephemeral-only modes.
[01:33] Lisa: 30 days is a lot for us; HIPAA-ish use cases — we prefer ephemeral or immediate deletion unless needed for debugging. do you support on-demand deletion APIs?
[01:44] Jamal: yes. there is a deletion API and an admin console action. we also support redact-before-log flows — you can tokenize or hash PHI client-side, or use server-side redactors we ship as reference code.
[01:55] Daniel: if we use the redact flow, is the raw prompt ever stored unredacted in any system?
[02:00] Priya: no, the idea is the redact step happens before persistent storage. there are short-lived caches (KV cache) for inference which are in-memory and not written to persistent logs, unless you opt into debugging logs.
[02:14] Anika: what about legal holds and subpoenas? deletion APIs are great but we may have to retain for a legal hold. how would that work?
[02:22] Maya: we honor lawful requests; if a legal hold is filed we can turn off deletion for that subset and provide an export. we also recommend customers manage their own copy for compliance where possible.
[02:32] Lisa: encryption — do you support customer-managed keys? we need control over keys for PHI workloads.
[02:36] Jamal: yes, BYOK is supported for Dedicated and Private. for hosted enterprise we offer BYOK backed by an HSM; keys never leave the HSM, and we perform envelope encryption. we support key rotation and provide logs of key usage.
[02:52] Daniel: do you have an example of how the BYOK flow looks? like when a request comes in is there an extra handshake? latency impact?
[02:59] Jamal: good point. BYOK introduces negligible latency for crypto ops — we cache DEKs (data encryption keys) in memory for the lifetime of the instance to avoid per-request HSM calls. there is a small startup cost on rotation. i'll follow up with an integration note that shows the handshake and sample curl/postman payloads.
[03:16] Anika: are keys accessible to Redwood employees? we need separation of duties.
[03:20] Priya: keys are customer-controlled in the sense that for BYOK they live in the customer's KMS/HSM or in a dedicated HSM managed per contract. Redwood maintains no plaintext copy. access controls at the platform layer prevent staff from exporting keys; any emergency access requires documented approvals and is logged.
[03:36] Lisa: audit logs — we need tamper-evident logs for both admin actions and API calls. retention minimum is 7 years in our policy. how flexible is audit log retention and who can view them?
[03:48] Maya: audit logs are configurable. we provide immutable append-only logs with chained hashes for tamper-evidence. retention can be configured per-customer up to X years (we'll include exact bounds in the SOC2 doc). auditor roles can be provisioned via RBAC and SAML assertions to your IdP so your auditors can read logs without needing Redwood accounts.
[04:06] Daniel: do you export logs to an S3 bucket in our account? that's our preferred model for long-term retention and integration with Splunk.
[04:12] Jamal: yes we can stream logs to a customer-owned S3 bucket, or to SIEM via syslog/Kinesis. that's the pattern we recommend for long retention and for forensic replay.
[04:22] Lisa: can we map your RBAC roles to our groups? like map Redwood "auditor" role to our "GCP-Audit" group via SAML?
[04:30] Priya: absolutely — we support SCIM provisioning, SAML assertions with role mapping, and just-in-time provisioning. we work with your IdP to map groups to Redwood roles and we can enforce least privilege defaults.
[04:42] Anika: about data residency — our data must never leave the EU for a subset of workloads. do you have region pinning?
[04:49] Maya: yes, we offer region pinning and Dedicated clusters in EU regions. for Private deployments we can support on-prem or VPC-connected setups within your cloud tenancy. we'll flag the differences between hosted->dedicated->private in the doc we share.
[05:02] Daniel: one more technical: where is KV cache stored and is it encrypted?
[05:06] Jamal: KV cache is in-memory and encrypted in transit between nodes; for Dedicated we can enable encrypted ram/cloud-provider-managed encryption-at-rest for swap if needed. it's not written to persistent logs.
[05:16] Lisa: do you maintain models or endpoints that could be shared across customers? we want to ensure cross-tenant leakage can't happen.
[05:23] Jamal: there are model shards and runtime instances — we logically isolate customer workloads. dedicated mode gives physical isolation. even in hosted mode we run multi-tenant pools but request data is isolated at the routing layer and never mixed into training pipelines.
[05:36] Anika: ok on 'no training', you said DPA covers it, is there an SOC2 control for that?
[05:41] Priya: SOC2 won't explicitly call out "no training" language but our SOC2 and ISO artefacts, plus contractual DPAs, provide the necessary attestations. we'll surface the DPA clause and the SOC2 report showing controls around data handling.
[05:55] Daniel: how do you handle telemetry/metrics for model perf? you mentioned pseudonymization earlier. can that be fully turned off?
[06:01] Jamal: telemetry can be opt-out for enterprise customers. we can provide metrics-only streams that carry no prompt text. for debugging you can run short time-limited debug captures under a controlled process.
[06:12] Lisa: is there a way to keep ephemeral mode but still get aggregate cost/usage telemetry? we need chargeback without PII.
[06:19] Maya: yes aggregated usage and token counts are retained separately from prompt content; usage metrics have no prompt context and are safe for chargeback.
[06:28] Anika: about deletion guarantees — after we call the deletion API how quickly is data actually removed? is there a verifiable proof?
[06:34] Jamal: deletion propagation is near-real-time for primary stores — typically within seconds to a few minutes. for backups it's tiered: snapshots may persist for a configurable window; we provide deletion attestations and logs showing deletion timestamp. for legal holds that would differ.
[06:49] Lisa: what about training guarantees if you ever onboard a new open model or partner — will data ever be used for that? we need contractual protection across future product changes.
[06:58] Priya: we include change-notice and customer consent clauses in the contract. any change that could affect training policy requires notice and an opt-in. for enterprise customers we can put stronger change control clauses into SOWs.
[07:12] Daniel: okay good. last one — do you support field-level redaction on the client side with your SDKs?
[07:18] Jamal: yes we provide client libraries with hooks for field-level redaction/tokenization and best-effort PII detectors. these are reference implementations; customers can use their own pre-processing too.
[07:30] Maya: quick recap — we will: 1) send SOC2 + ISO summary and pen test artifacts, 2) Jamal will share BYOK/KMS integration notes and example payloads, 3) we will propose pilot plan to validate ephemeral mode with synthetic PHI. any additions?
[07:45] Lisa: please include the exact retention limits for hosted vs dedicated vs private in writing.
[07:49] Anika: and provide the DPA excerpt that covers no-train language.
[07:52] Daniel: i'll send over the sample PHI patterns and a short list of endpoints we plan to hit in the pilot.
[07:59] Maya: perfect — we'll sync on dates in email. thanks everyone.
[08:04] Speaker 1: (end of recording)

Notes: transcription contains typical ASR errors — some acronyms mis-transcribed and speaker labels simplified. Redwood to follow up with secure links to docs (SOC2 pdf, DPA excerpt) and a pilot plan.

source.transcript:
Meeting header:
Date: 2025-09-09
Start time (UTC): 15:00
Duration: ~54 minutes
Attendees: Samira Patel (Redwood AE), Marcus Lin (Redwood SE), Anika Rao (Cerulean Head of Security), Jon Morales (Cerulean Platform Ops), Priya Desai (Cerulean Compliance)
Shared links: https://redwood.ai/docs/admin-export-playbook (deck), s3://redwood-samples/cerulean-export-sample.ndjson (sample)

Auto-summary (auto-generated, may be rough):
- Focus: what admin/admin-event logs are captured, export formats (ndjson/parquet), streaming vs batch exports, retention and archival options, encryption and KMS patterns, and proof/evidence for SOC2 controls.
- Actionable: Redwood to share schemas and run a pilot export to Cerulean's S3 bucket; Cerulean to confirm region and legal retention limits.

Topics: capture types; export destinations; retention & archival; encryption/KMS; RBAC/SSO linkage to events; evidence for audits

Transcript body:
[00:00] Samira: Hey everyone, thanks for making time. Quick intro, I'm Samira, AE on the account, Marcus is our solutions engineer, we'll walk through what admin events Redwood records and the export options.
[00:23] Anika: Thanks. High level — Cerulean needs a clear map of admin activity for SOC 2. We need evidence of who did what, when, and we need an exportable feed that can be stored offsite for 7 years if required.
[00:40] Marcus: Totally, and we should call out upfront — Redwood captures several admin event types: user lifecycle (create/update/delete), role changes, SSO auth events, API token creation/rotation, model rollout and config changes, key management operations, and system-level alerts. Some of those are instrumented at the control plane and others at the runtime plane.
[01:15] Jon: When you say "runtime plane" — can you clarify? Like actions that happen inside a private deployment vs on Redwood hosted?
[01:28] Marcus: Yes. For Redwood Hosted the control plane logs include request routing and admin UI changes. For Private/VPC deployments we ship a local agent that collects admin events and forwards them to your configured export endpoint; that agent logs locally and signs batches before export.
[01:48] Priya: Does the agent support offline buffering? Our on-prem environment sometimes loses connectivity for 12-48 hours.
[01:58] Marcus: Yes, the agent has local durable queueing with backpressure. It writes encrypted segments to disk, has a retry loop and will attempt ordered delivery to S3/Kafka once connectivity returns. There's an integrity checksum per segment.
[02:20] Anika: Can you describe the schema? We need field-level detail for our SIEM ingestion.
[02:28] Samira: We'll attach the schema, but quick summary: timestamp, event_id, actor_id (user or service), actor_type, action_type, resource_type, resource_id, previous_state (optional), new_state (optional), success/failure flag, ip_address, user_agent, request_id, trace_id, and metadata blob.
[02:55] Jon: That metadata blob — could that contain PII? We're sensitive about patient identifiers.
[03:03] Marcus: Good question. By default we avoid logging prompt contents or model outputs in admin events. Metadata can include benign routing tags and config diffs; anything that could include PII is redacted by default. You can also configure a strict "no-sensitive-metadata" mode which strips nonessential fields before export.
[03:24] Priya: How is redaction enforced? Is it client-side or server-side? We need demonstrable proof for audit.
[03:33] Marcus: We have a chain: server-side redactors plus the agent can apply additional scrubbing rules in Private deployments. For hosted customers we perform server-side redaction and can provide redaction logs showing scrubbed keys. For Private, you control the final scrubbing configuration in the agent and we provide signed manifests for each export batch.
[03:58] Anika: Signed manifests — what's the signing mechanism?
[04:03] Marcus: For hosted exports we generate signed export manifests using Redwood's KMS key (or customer-managed KMS on Dedicated/Private), producing an HMAC and optionally an X.509 signature if requested. For Private we can integrate with your HSM to sign manifests.
[04:25] Samira: Next, export destinations — we support direct S3 uploads (NDJSON or Parquet), streaming to Kafka, and HTTPS webhook pushes with retry/ack. We'll also do scheduled daily snapshots and on-demand manual exports.
[04:44] Jon: Which format do you recommend for SIEMs? NDJSON or Parquet?
[04:50] Marcus: NDJSON is easiest for line-based ingestion (Splunk, ELK). Parquet is more efficient for large-scale analytics and cheaper to store long-term. We provide schema-typed Parquet with partitioning by date and region.
[05:09] Priya: If we choose Parquet for cold storage, do you provide tooling to convert from NDJSON?
[05:15] Marcus: Yes — Redwood Optimize has a conversion utility in the export playbook; we can either produce Parquet directly or convert post-export.
[05:25] Anika: On retention — Cerulean's legal says 7 years for some audit material. What are your retention posture options?
[05:33] Samira: Redwood itself retains admin telemetry in the control plane for 90 days by default for hosted; longer retention is available as an add-on (1,3,7 years). For Dedicated/Private you control retention locally; our agent can implement TTL and cold archival to Glacier-equivalent via your config.
[05:53] Jon: So for Hosted if we need 7 years, Redwood will ship an export to our S3 and then not necessarily keep a copy unless we pay for extended retention?
[06:02] Samira: Correct. By default the control plane keeps 90 days. For compliance you should be exporting to your offsite storage. We can provide a managed archival add-on but most customers export to customer-managed S3/GCS.
[06:16] Priya: Encryption — we need CMEK across at-rest and in-transit, and HSM key wrapping for long-term evidence. How do you support that?
[06:27] Marcus: For in-transit it's TLS 1.2+ by default. At-rest, Hosted uses Redwood-managed KMS with envelope encryption; we offer CMEK for Dedicated and Private deployments so you supply the key id. For extra controls we support integration with customer HSMs for on-prem signing and key wrapping.
[06:48] Anika: If we request CMEK for Hosted, do you bring the key into Redwood or is it externally controlled?
[06:55] Marcus: For Hosted we do a Bring Your Own Key (BYOK) flow where the key material remains in your cloud KMS (e.g., AWS KMS) and Redwood receives delegated encryption tokens. Redwood never stores raw key material. For Dedicated/Private keys remain in your environment.
[07:10] Jon: Is there double encryption or tenant-separating keys per account?
[07:15] Marcus: Yes, we create per-tenant envelope keys and also per-export signing keys if requested, so evidence can't be mixed across tenants.
[07:26] Anika: About integrity — auditors like to see tamper evidence. Do you provide immutable log chains or Merkle proofs?
[07:35] Marcus: We provide optional append-only logs with hash chaining and a daily root hash. For hosted customers we can publish a signed root hash; for Private you can store the log locally and we can sign the daily roots with your HSM. That gives you tamper-evidence for exported batches.
[07:55] Priya: Do you include user session context for SSO events? Like original SAML assertions or just the derived identity?
[08:03] Marcus: We capture derived identity, SAML transaction id, assertion id (not the full assertion payload), and auth method. Full assertions are not stored by default but can be if you opt in with a special retention and encryption profile.
[08:19] Anika: Can we get evidence mapping between UI actions and API calls? It's important to show that UI-driven role changes and API-driven role changes generate the same audit event.
[08:28] Marcus: Yes — every admin event includes a source field: UI, API, automation. The schema normalizes actor id and includes request_id and trace_id to correlate.
[08:40] Jon: For private deployments, where does the agent send exports? Only to customer endpoints?
[08:46] Marcus: The agent is configurable — you can point it to customer S3, your Kafka cluster, or to a secured FedRAMP-similar endpoint. We also support dual-write (local archive + push to Redwood for central observability) with clear opt-in.
[09:02] Priya: Dual-write worries us from a data residency perspective. If data must not leave region, dual-write can't be used.
[09:09] Marcus: Understood. Dual-write is opt-in and we can enforce region constraints; the agent respects the region setting and will not route outside the allowed endpoints.
[09:20] Samira: Quick pause — any questions before we get into example exports?
[09:24] Anika: Go ahead.
[09:26] Marcus: We'll walk through a sample NDJSON. (screen share) Example event: {"timestamp":"2025-09-08T22:12:03Z","event_id":"evt_...","actor_id":"user:anj-123","actor_type":"user","action_type":"role.update","resource_type":"project","resource_id":"proj-77","previous_state":{"roles":["viewer"]},"new_state":{"roles":["editor","viewer"]},"source":"ui","ip_address":"52.23.11.7","user_agent":"Mozilla/5.0...","request_id":"req-abc","trace_id":"tr-123","metadata":{}}
Note: inline JSON is simplified in the transcript.
[10:10] Anika: That shows the role delta — good. How do you handle large diffs? For instance a config change that flips numerous fields — do you capture the full previous/new state?
[10:22] Marcus: We capture diffs up to a configurable payload size. By default we limit the previous_state/new_state to 32KB; larger diffs are stored as a separate artifact referenced by an object id in the event. The artifact is encrypted and archived.
[10:38] Jon: About exports — how do you authenticate to our S3? Do you upload using our IAM role or do we grant a cross-account role?
[10:46] Marcus: We support both patterns. Preferred is to create a cross-account IAM role with a narrow trust relationship and let Redwood assume it to upload. Alternatively you can provide a presigned bucket endpoint for push. For Kafka we support SASL/SSL and mTLS for mutual TLS auth.
[11:03] Priya: Auditors will ask for retention deletion proofs. Can you produce deletion manifests showing when an object was TTL'd and who triggered purge?
[11:13] Marcus: Yes — deletion events are recorded as admin events with reason, triggered_by, and proof object id. For Hosted managed archival we also provide a retention log that shows TTL expirations and manual deletions, with signed manifests.
[11:30] Anika: What about monitoring — can we subscribe to alerts for suspicious admin activity?
[11:36] Samira: Redwood Console allows alerting on admin event patterns — role escalations, multiple failed token rotations, unusual IPs. Alerts can go to email, PagerDuty, or webhooks. You can also stream events to your SIEM for rule matching.
[11:52] Jon: Is there an out-of-the-box correlation pack for Splunk?
[11:56] Marcus: We ship a Splunk app and an Elastic ingest pipeline as part of the export playbook. We'll share those artifacts.
[12:06] Priya: Are there examples of customer-driven retention policies — like legal wants 7 years but security wants 90 days readily available — how do you support tiered retention?
[12:18] Marcus: Tiered retention is supported. Short-term hot store (90 days) is indexable in the Console; older data is moved to cold Parquet partitions and then to Glacier-equivalent. We can also do an immutable vault for a set of evidence objects.
[12:34] Anika: Auditors sometimes want signed daily summaries rather than full logs. Is that an option?
[12:40] Marcus: Yes — we can produce daily summary manifests with counts by event_type, top actors, and a signed root hash of the full day's exports. That keeps audit review simpler without sending terabytes.
[12:55] Samira: Quick note on RBAC mapping: audit events include role assignments and the role hierarchy snapshot. If you use our SSO integration, we also log identity provider events (SAML/SCIM provisioning) and mapping rules.
[13:14] Jon: If we use SCIM auto-provisioning, can we get a reconcile report showing accounts that exist in IdP but not in Redwood and vice versa?
[13:23] Marcus: Yes, reconcile artifacts are generated weekly and a reconcile diff event is emitted. We can export that as CSV to your bucket.
[13:34] Anika: For KMS logs — how much detail from KMS operations is exposed? We need to show key rotation events and who rotated keys.
[13:42] Marcus: We record key rotation triggers, the key id after rotation, rotation schedule changes, and KMS access attempts. For Hosted CMEK flows we record the fact that the BYOK key was used, rotation timestamps, and we can include the KMS operation ids. The actual key material is never logged.
[14:00] Priya: If we want HSM-backed signing for exports, what's the integration model?
[14:06] Marcus: For Private deployments we can integrate with your HSM via PKCS#11 or use an HSM proxy. We sign manifests with a key defined in your HSM; we provide sample configuration in the playbook.
[14:20] Anika: Can you show an example of a signed manifest?
[14:23] Marcus: Example manifest header: {"date":"2025-09-08","export_id":"exp-...","signed_by":"redwood:signing-key-1","signature":"MIIBIj...","hash":"sha256:...","files":[{"path":"s3://...","hash":"...","size":...}]}
[14:50] Jon: What about retention enforcement for artifacts that are referenced by events? If we delete an artifact, does the event still reference a dead object id?
[15:00] Marcus: Events reference object ids and deletion is recorded as a mutation event. If the artifact no longer exists the event will show a 410 reference and the deletion manifest will provide the reason and proof of deletion.
[15:16] Priya: We need proofs that log exports haven't been tampered with during transfer. How do you provide chain-of-custody?
[15:23] Marcus: Chain-of-custody includes signed manifests, transport TLS with mutual auth, and per-segment checksums recorded in the manifest. For extra assurance we can push the daily root hash to a transparency log or to an external witness.
[15:42] Anika: For data residency mapping — we need a report that shows where each export landed and the region-level residency.
[15:49] Marcus: We can provide an export registry with per-batch metadata: destination region, bucket arn, export start/end, signer, and root hash. That registry is itself signed and can be included in audits.
[16:05] Samira: Switching to compliance evidence — auditors ask for sample log bundles. We provide a sample bundle generator that pulls a date range and zips signed manifests, event NDJSON/Parquet, and a summary report.
[16:22] Priya: Are the sample bundles anonymized by default?
[16:27] Marcus: By default they reflect production content but you can request anonymized bundles for external sharing; anonymization replaces actor ids with stable pseudonyms and strips PII per rules.
[16:40] Anika: On legal holds — if litigation requires holding some artifacts beyond retention, can we place an export under hold?
[16:47] Marcus: Yes, we support legal hold flags on exported artifacts. For hosted customers you can request Redwood to mark exports as immutable and prevent TTL expiry; for Private you can enforce it locally in the agent.
[17:00] Jon: What's the smallest export granularity? Per-event, hourly batches, daily snapshots?
[17:06] Marcus: We support per-event streaming, minute/hourly batches, and daily snapshots. You can configure the batching frequency.
[17:16] Priya: For high-volume tenants, what's your recommended partition scheme?
[17:21] Marcus: Partition by date and tenant_id and optionally by resource_type. For Parquet we recommend daily partitions with subfolders by region.
[17:32] Anika: Are there any caveats around PII showing up in logs — especially in metadata blobs that are generated by downstream connectors?
[17:40] Marcus: We advise treating metadata as untrusted; the export agent can run a rule engine that redacts patterns like PHI/SSNs and can replace with tokenized references. We can provide a regex pack and integrate with your DLP tools.
[17:55] Jon: For the pilot run, what's the expected throughput? We want to test a week of events compressed.
[18:01] Marcus: For a medium tenant (few hundred admins) event volume is moderate; agent throughput to S3 is typically tens to low hundreds MB/s depending on network. We'll provide a benchmark run.
[18:15] Samira: We'll include a small benchmark artifact in the playbook with sample rates and sizing.
[18:22] Anika: For audited access to logs — can we do read-only signed access for auditors?
[18:28] Marcus: Yes, we can issue time-limited signed URLs or grant a read-only cross-account role for auditors with MFA requirement.
[18:38] Priya: A few compliance-specific questions — do you have SOC 2 evidence packages for exports?
[18:44] Samira: We have a SOC 2 Type II report that covers logging, retention, and incident response. For customer-specific evidence we package export manifests, signed roots, and retention logs into an evidence bundle that maps to control objectives.
[18:58] Jon: Would you be willing to sign a data processing amendment that specifically references export handling and retention?
[19:05] Samira: Yes — legal has templates; we'll start with the standard DPA and add the export/retention annex.
[19:14] Anika: About monitoring chain — if there's a suspected tamper, how fast can we verify?
[19:20] Marcus: With signed root hashes and manifest history you can run a verification script that checks all signatures and hashes; for a day it's typically under a minute to verify root integrity, full rehash depends on volume.
[19:35] Priya: Are there particular limitations on Private agent where some events are only available in control plane?
[19:42] Marcus: A handful of CC-hosted operational telemetry (like multi-tenant scheduler metrics) don't ship in Private by default for privacy. Admin events relevant to the tenant are available locally.
[19:55] Jon: For cross-tenant troubleshooting, do you provide redacted records?
[19:59] Marcus: We can produce redacted aggregated events with tenant tags removed — useful for debugging without exposing tenant data.
[20:10] Samira: We're about a third through, want to keep moving to concrete next steps and the pilot outline.
[20:14] Anika: Yes please.
[20:16] Marcus: Pilot outline: 1) Exchange S3 bucket details and cross-account role, 2) Run a 48-72 hour streaming export of admin events, 3) Validate manifests, verify checksums and signatures, 4) Run retention/TTL simulation by marking some artifacts as expired, 5) Produce a small evidence bundle for Cerulean auditors.
[20:40] Jon: For step 4, can the agent simulate TTL expiry without actually deleting? We want to see deletion manifests first.
[20:49] Marcus: Yes — there's a dry-run mode that produces deletion manifests but doesn't remove objects until you confirm.
[20:58] Priya: Timeframe? We want this before our audit kickoff on 2025-10-01.

title: Security review: admin event exports & retention — Cerulean Health
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2025-09-09
Start time (UTC): 15:00
Duration: ~54 minutes
Attendees: Samira Patel (Redwood AE), Marcus Lin (Redwood SE), Anika Rao (Cerulean Head of Security), Jon Morales (Cerulean Platform Ops), Priya Desai (Cerulean Compliance)
Shared links: https://redwood.ai/docs/admin-export-playbook (deck), s3://redwood-samples/cerulean-export-sample.ndjson (sample)

Auto-summary (auto-generated, may be rough):
- Focus: what admin/admin-event logs are captured, export formats (ndjson/parquet), streaming vs batch exports, retention and archival options, encryption and KMS patterns, and proof/evidence for SOC2 controls.
- Actionable: Redwood to share schemas and run a pilot export to Cerulean's S3 bucket; Cerulean to confirm region and legal retention limits.

Topics: capture types; export destinations; retention & archival; encryption/KMS; RBAC/SSO linkage to events; evidence for audits

Transcript body:
[00:00] Samira: Hey everyone, thanks for making time. Quick intro, I'm Samira, AE on the account, Marcus is our solutions engineer, we'll walk through what admin events Redwood records and the export options.
[00:23] Anika: Thanks. High level — Cerulean needs a clear map of admin activity for SOC 2. We need evidence of who did what, when, and we need an exportable feed that can be stored offsite for 7 years if required.
[00:40] Marcus: Totally, and we should call out upfront — Redwood captures several admin event types: user lifecycle (create/update/delete), role changes, SSO auth events, API token creation/rotation, model rollout and config changes, key management operations, and system-level alerts. Some of those are instrumented at the control plane and others at the runtime plane.
[01:15] Jon: When you say "runtime plane" — can you clarify? Like actions that happen inside a private deployment vs on Redwood hosted?
[01:28] Marcus: Yes. For Redwood Hosted the control plane logs include request routing and admin UI changes. For Private/VPC deployments we ship a local agent that collects admin events and forwards them to your configured export endpoint; that agent logs locally and signs batches before export.
[01:48] Priya: Does the agent support offline buffering? Our on-prem environment sometimes loses connectivity for 12-48 hours.
[01:58] Marcus: Yes, the agent has local durable queueing with backpressure. It writes encrypted segments to disk, has a retry loop and will attempt ordered delivery to S3/Kafka once connectivity returns. There's an integrity checksum per segment.
[02:20] Anika: Can you describe the schema? We need field-level detail for our SIEM ingestion.
[02:28] Samira: We'll attach the schema, but quick summary: timestamp, event_id, actor_id (user or service), actor_type, action_type, resource_type, resource_id, previous_state (optional), new_state (optional), success/failure flag, ip_address, user_agent, request_id, trace_id, and metadata blob.
[02:55] Jon: That metadata blob — could that contain PII? We're sensitive about patient identifiers.
[03:03] Marcus: Good question. By default we avoid logging prompt contents or model outputs in admin events. Metadata can include benign routing tags and config diffs; anything that could include PII is redacted by default. You can also configure a strict "no-sensitive-metadata" mode which strips nonessential fields before export.
[03:24] Priya: How is redaction enforced? Is it client-side or server-side? We need demonstrable proof for audit.
[03:33] Marcus: We have a chain: server-side redactors plus the agent can apply additional scrubbing rules in Private deployments. For hosted customers we perform server-side redaction and can provide redaction logs showing scrubbed keys. For Private, you control the final scrubbing configuration in the agent and we provide signed manifests for each export batch.
[03:58] Anika: Signed manifests — what's the signing mechanism?
[04:03] Marcus: For hosted exports we generate signed export manifests using Redwood's KMS key (or customer-managed KMS on Dedicated/Private), producing an HMAC and optionally an X.509 signature if requested. For Private we can integrate with your HSM to sign manifests.
[04:25] Samira: Next, export destinations — we support direct S3 uploads (NDJSON or Parquet), streaming to Kafka, and HTTPS webhook pushes with retry/ack. We'll also do scheduled daily snapshots and on-demand manual exports.
[04:44] Jon: Which format do you recommend for SIEMs? NDJSON or Parquet?
[04:50] Marcus: NDJSON is easiest for line-based ingestion (Splunk, ELK). Parquet is more efficient for large-scale analytics and cheaper to store long-term. We provide schema-typed Parquet with partitioning by date and region.
[05:09] Priya: If we choose Parquet for cold storage, do you provide tooling to convert from NDJSON?
[05:15] Marcus: Yes — Redwood Optimize has a conversion utility in the export playbook; we can either produce Parquet directly or convert post-export.
[05:25] Anika: On retention — Cerulean's legal says 7 years for some audit material. What are your retention posture options?
[05:33] Samira: Redwood itself retains admin telemetry in the control plane for 90 days by default for hosted; longer retention is available as an add-on (1,3,7 years). For Dedicated/Private you control retention locally; our agent can implement TTL and cold archival to Glacier-equivalent via your config.
[05:53] Jon: So for Hosted if we need 7 years, Redwood will ship an export to our S3 and then not necessarily keep a copy unless we pay for extended retention?
[06:02] Samira: Correct. By default the control plane keeps 90 days. For compliance you should be exporting to your offsite storage. We can provide a managed archival add-on but most customers export to customer-managed S3/GCS.
[06:16] Priya: Encryption — we need CMEK across at-rest and in-transit, and HSM key wrapping for long-term evidence. How do you support that?
[06:27] Marcus: For in-transit it's TLS 1.2+ by default. At-rest, Hosted uses Redwood-managed KMS with envelope encryption; we offer CMEK for Dedicated and Private deployments so you supply the key id. For extra controls we support integration with customer HSMs for on-prem signing and key wrapping.
[06:48] Anika: If we request CMEK for Hosted, do you bring the key into Redwood or is it externally controlled?
[06:55] Marcus: For Hosted we do a Bring Your Own Key (BYOK) flow where the key material remains in your cloud KMS (e.g., AWS KMS) and Redwood receives delegated encryption tokens. Redwood never stores raw key material. For Dedicated/Private keys remain in your environment.
[07:10] Jon: Is there double encryption or tenant-separating keys per account?
[07:15] Marcus: Yes, we create per-tenant envelope keys and also per-export signing keys if requested, so evidence can't be mixed across tenants.
[07:26] Anika: About integrity — auditors like to see tamper evidence. Do you provide immutable log chains or Merkle proofs?
[07:35] Marcus: We provide optional append-only logs with hash chaining and a daily root hash. For hosted customers we can publish a signed root hash; for Private you can store the log locally and we can sign the daily roots with your HSM. That gives you tamper-evidence for exported batches.
[07:55] Priya: Do you include user session context for SSO events? Like original SAML assertions or just the derived identity?
[08:03] Marcus: We capture derived identity, SAML transaction id, assertion id (not the full assertion payload), and auth method. Full assertions are not stored by default but can be if you opt in with a special retention and encryption profile.
[08:19] Anika: Can we get evidence mapping between UI actions and API calls? It's important to show that UI-driven role changes and API-driven role changes generate the same audit event.
[08:28] Marcus: Yes — every admin event includes a source field: UI, API, automation. The schema normalizes actor id and includes request_id and trace_id to correlate.
[08:40] Jon: For private deployments, where does the agent send exports? Only to customer endpoints?
[08:46] Marcus: The agent is configurable — you can point it to customer S3, your Kafka cluster, or to a secured FedRAMP-similar endpoint. We also support dual-write (local archive + push to Redwood for central observability) with clear opt-in.
[09:02] Priya: Dual-write worries us from a data residency perspective. If data must not leave region, dual-write can't be used.
[09:09] Marcus: Understood. Dual-write is opt-in and we can enforce region constraints; the agent respects the region setting and will not route outside the allowed endpoints.
[09:20] Samira: Quick pause — any questions before we get into example exports?
[09:24] Anika: Go ahead.
[09:26] Marcus: We'll walk through a sample NDJSON. (screen share) Example event: {"timestamp":"2025-09-08T22:12:03Z","event_id":"evt_...","actor_id":"user:anj-123","actor_type":"user","action_type":"role.update","resource_type":"project","resource_id":"proj-77","previous_state":{"roles":["viewer"]},"new_state":{"roles":["editor","viewer"]},"source":"ui","ip_address":"52.23.11.7","user_agent":"Mozilla/5.0...","request_id":"req-abc","trace_id":"tr-123","metadata":{}}
Note: inline JSON is simplified in the transcript.
[10:10] Anika: That shows the role delta — good. How do you handle large diffs? For instance a config change that flips numerous fields — do you capture the full previous/new state?
[10:22] Marcus: We capture diffs up to a configurable payload size. By default we limit the previous_state/new_state to 32KB; larger diffs are stored as a separate artifact referenced by an object id in the event. The artifact is encrypted and archived.
[10:38] Jon: About exports — how do you authenticate to our S3? Do you upload using our IAM role or do we grant a cross-account role?
[10:46] Marcus: We support both patterns. Preferred is to create a cross-account IAM role with a narrow trust relationship and let Redwood assume it to upload. Alternatively you can provide a presigned bucket endpoint for push. For Kafka we support SASL/SSL and mTLS for mutual TLS auth.
[11:03] Priya: Auditors will ask for retention deletion proofs. Can you produce deletion manifests showing when an object was TTL'd and who triggered purge?
[11:13] Marcus: Yes — deletion events are recorded as admin events with reason, triggered_by, and proof object id. For Hosted managed archival we also provide a retention log that shows TTL expirations and manual deletions, with signed manifests.
[11:30] Anika: What about monitoring — can we subscribe to alerts for suspicious admin activity?
[11:36] Samira: Redwood Console allows alerting on admin event patterns — role escalations, multiple failed token rotations, unusual IPs. Alerts can go to email, PagerDuty, or webhooks. You can also stream events to your SIEM for rule matching.
[11:52] Jon: Is there an out-of-the-box correlation pack for Splunk?
[11:56] Marcus: We ship a Splunk app and an Elastic ingest pipeline as part of the export playbook. We'll share those artifacts.
[12:06] Priya: Are there examples of customer-driven retention policies — like legal wants 7 years but security wants 90 days readily available — how do you support tiered retention?
[12:18] Marcus: Tiered retention is supported. Short-term hot store (90 days) is indexable in the Console; older data is moved to cold Parquet partitions and then to Glacier-equivalent. We can also do an immutable vault for a set of evidence objects.
[12:34] Anika: Auditors sometimes want signed daily summaries rather than full logs. Is that an option?
[12:40] Marcus: Yes — we can produce daily summary manifests with counts by event_type, top actors, and a signed root hash of the full day's exports. That keeps audit review simpler without sending terabytes.
[12:55] Samira: Quick note on RBAC mapping: audit events include role assignments and the role hierarchy snapshot. If you use our SSO integration, we also log identity provider events (SAML/SCIM provisioning) and mapping rules.
[13:14] Jon: If we use SCIM auto-provisioning, can we get a reconcile report showing accounts that exist in IdP but not in Redwood and vice versa?
[13:23] Marcus: Yes, reconcile artifacts are generated weekly and a reconcile diff event is emitted. We can export that as CSV to your bucket.
[13:34] Anika: For KMS logs — how much detail from KMS operations is exposed? We need to show key rotation events and who rotated keys.
[13:42] Marcus: We record key rotation triggers, the key id after rotation, rotation schedule changes, and KMS access attempts. For Hosted CMEK flows we record the fact that the BYOK key was used, rotation timestamps, and we can include the KMS operation ids. The actual key material is never logged.
[14:00] Priya: If we want HSM-backed signing for exports, what's the integration model?
[14:06] Marcus: For Private deployments we can integrate with your HSM via PKCS#11 or use an HSM proxy. We sign manifests with a key defined in your HSM; we provide sample configuration in the playbook.
[14:20] Anika: Can you show an example of a signed manifest?
[14:23] Marcus: Example manifest header: {"date":"2025-09-08","export_id":"exp-...","signed_by":"redwood:signing-key-1","signature":"MIIBIj...","hash":"sha256:...","files":[{"path":"s3://...","hash":"...","size":...}]}
[14:50] Jon: What about retention enforcement for artifacts that are referenced by events? If we delete an artifact, does the event still reference a dead object id?
[15:00] Marcus: Events reference object ids and deletion is recorded as a mutation event. If the artifact no longer exists the event will show a 410 reference and the deletion manifest will provide the reason and proof of deletion.
[15:16] Priya: We need proofs that log exports haven't been tampered with during transfer. How do you provide chain-of-custody?
[15:23] Marcus: Chain-of-custody includes signed manifests, transport TLS with mutual auth, and per-segment checksums recorded in the manifest. For extra assurance we can push the daily root hash to a transparency log or to an external witness.
[15:42] Anika: For data residency mapping — we need a report that shows where each export landed and the region-level residency.
[15:49] Marcus: We can provide an export registry with per-batch metadata: destination region, bucket arn, export start/end, signer, and root hash. That registry is itself signed and can be included in audits.
[16:05] Samira: Switching to compliance evidence — auditors ask for sample log bundles. We provide a sample bundle generator that pulls a date range and zips signed manifests, event NDJSON/Parquet, and a summary report.
[16:22] Priya: Are the sample bundles anonymized by default?
[16:27] Marcus: By default they reflect production content but you can request anonymized bundles for external sharing; anonymization replaces actor ids with stable pseudonyms and strips PII per rules.
[16:40] Anika: On legal holds — if litigation requires holding some artifacts beyond retention, can we place an export under hold?
[16:47] Marcus: Yes, we support legal hold flags on exported artifacts. For hosted customers you can request Redwood to mark exports as immutable and prevent TTL expiry; for Private you can enforce it locally in the agent.
[17:00] Jon: What's the smallest export granularity? Per-event, hourly batches, daily snapshots?
[17:06] Marcus: We support per-event streaming, minute/hourly batches, and daily snapshots. You can configure the batching frequency.
[17:16] Priya: For high-volume tenants, what's your recommended partition scheme?
[17:21] Marcus: Partition by date and tenant_id and optionally by resource_type. For Parquet we recommend daily partitions with subfolders by region.
[17:32] Anika: Are there any caveats around PII showing up in logs — especially in metadata blobs that are generated by downstream connectors?
[17:40] Marcus: We advise treating metadata as untrusted; the export agent can run a rule engine that redacts patterns like PHI/SSNs and can replace with tokenized references. We can provide a regex pack and integrate with your DLP tools.
[17:55] Jon: For the pilot run, what's the expected throughput? We want to test a week of events compressed.
[18:01] Marcus: For a medium tenant (few hundred admins) event volume is moderate; agent throughput to S3 is typically tens to low hundreds MB/s depending on network. We'll provide a benchmark run.
[18:15] Samira: We'll include a small benchmark artifact in the playbook with sample rates and sizing.
[18:22] Anika: For audited access to logs — can we do read-only signed access for auditors?
[18:28] Marcus: Yes, we can issue time-limited signed URLs or grant a read-only cross-account role for auditors with MFA requirement.
[18:38] Priya: A few compliance-specific questions — do you have SOC 2 evidence packages for exports?
[18:44] Samira: We have a SOC 2 Type II report that covers logging, retention, and incident response. For customer-specific evidence we package export manifests, signed roots, and retention logs into an evidence bundle that maps to control objectives.
[18:58] Jon: Would you be willing to sign a data processing amendment that specifically references export handling and retention?
[19:05] Samira: Yes — legal has templates; we'll start with the standard DPA and add the export/retention annex.
[19:14] Anika: About monitoring chain — if there's a suspected tamper, how fast can we verify?
[19:20] Marcus: With signed root hashes and manifest history you can run a verification script that checks all signatures and hashes; for a day it's typically under a minute to verify root integrity, full rehash depends on volume.
[19:35] Priya: Are there particular limitations on Private agent where some events are only available in control plane?
[19:42] Marcus: A handful of CC-hosted operational telemetry (like multi-tenant scheduler metrics) don't ship in Private by default for privacy. Admin events relevant to the tenant are available locally.
[19:55] Jon: For cross-tenant troubleshooting, do you provide redacted records?
[19:59] Marcus: We can produce redacted aggregated events with tenant tags removed — useful for debugging without exposing tenant data.
[20:10] Samira: We're about a third through, want to keep moving to concrete next steps and the pilot outline.
[20:14] Anika: Yes please.
[20:16] Marcus: Pilot outline: 1) Exchange S3 bucket details and cross-account role, 2) Run a 48-72 hour streaming export of admin events, 3) Validate manifests, verify checksums and signatures, 4) Run retention/TTL simulation by marking some artifacts as expired, 5) Produce a small evidence bundle for Cerulean auditors.

[04:25] Samira: Next, export destinations — we support direct S3 uploads (NDJSON or Parquet), streaming to Kafka, and HTTPS webhook pushes with retry/ack. We'll also do scheduled daily snapshots and on-demand manual exports.
